text: Nitride phosphors are essential components for high-performance white light-emitting diodes. In this study, we demonstrate how cation substitution can be used to engineer the local structure of (Sr,Ca)AlSiN3:Eu phosphors, inducing specific charge variations and a rearrangement of neighboring nitride clusters to enhance luminescent performance. Replacing Ca²⁺ with Sr²⁺ expands the lattice volume and the bc-plane while simultaneously compressing the interlayer distance along the a-axis. This structural distortion creates high-coordination environments and sufficient spatial freedom around the Sr/Eu centers, which facilitates the reduction of activator ions as evidenced by X-ray absorption near-edge structure (XANES) spectroscopy. Consequently, the modified phosphors exhibit a blue-shifted emission and a greater than 10% increase in photoluminescent intensity. High-resolution transmission electron microscopy and solid-state Raman spectra further reveal that cation substitution triggers a unique local reorganization of the anion environment. Solid-state nuclear magnetic resonance (NMR) spectroscopy confirms a specific redistribution of (Si/Al)N₄ tetrahedra surrounding the divalent cation sites. This tailored structural ordering results in a more rigid framework, significantly improving thermal stability; specifically, the thermal quenching resistance at 473 K increased by 10% for the Sr-rich composition (x = 0.9) compared to the Sr-free sample. This work provides critical insights into how the neighbor sequence of selective tetrahedral sites—such as those involving Li, Mg, Al, or Si—can be manipulated to optimize the properties of nitride-based materials.
